coredata.dc:title: Hierarchical design of carbon nanofibers wrapped antimony selenide nanorods with heteroatom-doped carbon quantum dots as efficient anode material of high-performance sodium-ion batteries

coredata.dc:description: Researchers have focused on sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) due to low precursor costs and abundant reserves. However, low efficiency of anode materials hinders SIB commercialization, mainly due to large ionic radius and slow kinetics of Na+. Metal selenides such as Sb2Se3 have regarded as promising anode materials of SIBs due to high theoretical capacities. Despite this, the practical application has been limited by the poor rate capability, conductivity, and stability. In this study, one-dimensional Sb2Se3 nanorods and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) combined with carbon nanofibers (CNFs) have been developed as an effective anode material of SIBs. The uniform distribution of N-CQDs in the Sb2Se3 coated CNFs (Sb2Se3/CNFs) anode results in the highest reversible capacity of 647.9 mAhg−1 at 0.05 Ag-1. Moreover, the Sb2Se3 and N-CQDs coated CNFs (Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs) anode presents a good rate performance and retains a high reversible capacity of 444.7 mAhg−1 at a high current density of 0.4 Ag-1. Additionally, the Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs anode demonstrates excellent stability, retaining 78.8% of the initial capacity after 100 cycles. The improved electrochemical performance is attributed to low volume expansion and good conductivity due to carbon coating and addition of N-CQDs. Galvanostatic intermittent titration technique is also used to examine Na+ diffusion coefficients.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of heteroatom-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
Material characterizations of Sb2Se3, CQDs, Sb2Se3/CNFs, Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs and Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Science and Technology Council (NSTC) in Taiwan, under Grant 111-2221-E-027-071-MY3 and 113-2221-E-027-010-MY3. This work is also assisted by Precision Analysis and Material Research Center, NTUT.

On the other hand, carbon modification is a promising and widely used method to improve the sodium-storage capabilities of anode materials [36]. Significantly, the carbon coating on metal selenide-based anodes can enhance the electrochemical performance by improving electronic conductivity and reducing the volume changes of the anodes. For instance, Hu et al. synthesized Sb2Se3/rGO nanocomposites for the production of a low-temperature anode material, showing good rate capability and stability [34]. Wang et al. demonstrated a sandwich-like SnSe2/reduced graphene oxide (rGO) as an anode material of SIBs, which exhibited an initial Coulombic efficiency (ICE) of 73.7% and a capacity retention of 86.2% at 0.1 Ag\u22121 after 150 cycles [37]. Previous studies have shown that the addition of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can improve the electrochemical performance of SIBs [38,39]. This sort of nanomaterials can offer several advantages, such as abundant electrochemically active sites, large contact areas with electrolytes, high capacities, superior structural stability, and surface strain resistance supporting shortened Na+ transition distance [40]. To the best of our knowledge, the Sb2Se3/CQDs composites have not been reported for use in the fabrication of SIB anodes. It is promising to explore the feasibility of applying Sb2Se3/CQDs composites as the anode material of SIBs with high performance.This work was supported by National Science and Technology Council (NSTC) in Taiwan, under Grant 111-2221-E-027-071-MY3 and 113-2221-E-027-010-MY3. This work is also assisted by Precision Analysis and Material Research Center, NTUT.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The SEM images of (a) Sb2Se3-18 h, (b) Sb2Se3-24 h, (c) Sb2Se3-30 h, and (d) Sb2Se3-36 h; (e) schematic of the growth mechanism for Sb2Se3.

The SEM images of (a,d) Sb2Se3/CNFs, (b,e) Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs, and (c,f) Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs; the TEM images of (g) Sb2Se3, (h) N-CQDs, (i) N, S-CQDs, (j) Sb2Se3/CNFs, (k) Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs and (l) Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs.

(a) XRD patterns of Sb2Se3 synthesized at different hydrothermal durations; (b) XRD patterns, (c) Raman spectra and (d) XPS survey spectra of Sb2Se3, Sb2Se3/CNFs, Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs and Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs; XPS spectra of (e) Sb 3d + O1s, (f) Se 3d, (g) C 1s, and (h) N 1s for Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs.

The CV curves of (a) Sb2Se3, (b) Sb2Se3/CNFs, (c) Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs, and (d) Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs anodes recorded at 0.1 mV/s in the potential window of 0–2.5 V vs. Na+/Na; the CV curves of (e) Sb2Se3, (f) Sb2Se3/CNFs, (g) Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs and (h) Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs anodes recorded at different scan rates in the potential window of 0–2.5 V vs. Na+/Na. The GC/D curves of (i) Sb2Se3, (j) Sb2Se3/CNFs, (k) Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs, and (l) Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs anodes recorded at 0.2 A/g.
(a) Cycling stability, Nyquist plots after (b) the 1st cycle and (c) after the 100th cycle, and (d) GITT curves of Sb2Se3, Sb2Se3/CNFs, Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs, and Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs anodes.
Partial lists of the rate capability and cycling stability of Sb, Sb2Se3, and Sb2Se3/carbon -based anode materials reported in previous literatures and this work.

head.ce:title: Hierarchical design of carbon nanofibers wrapped antimony selenide nanorods with heteroatom-doped carbon quantum dots as efficient anode material of high-performance sodium-ion batteries

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Researchers have focused on sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) due to low precursor costs and abundant reserves. However, low efficiency of anode materials hinders SIB commercialization, mainly due to large ionic radius and slow kinetics of Na+. Metal selenides such as Sb2Se3 have regarded as promising anode materials of SIBs due to high theoretical capacities. Despite this, the practical application has been limited by the poor rate capability, conductivity, and stability. In this study, one-dimensional Sb2Se3 nanorods and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) combined with carbon nanofibers (CNFs) have been developed as an effective anode material of SIBs. The uniform distribution of N-CQDs in the Sb2Se3 coated CNFs (Sb2Se3/CNFs) anode results in the highest reversible capacity of 647.9 mAhg−1 at 0.05 Ag-1. Moreover, the Sb2Se3 and N-CQDs coated CNFs (Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs) anode presents a good rate performance and retains a high reversible capacity of 444.7 mAhg−1 at a high current density of 0.4 Ag-1. Additionally, the Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs anode demonstrates excellent stability, retaining 78.8% of the initial capacity after 100 cycles. The improved electrochemical performance is attributed to low volume expansion and good conductivity due to carbon coating and addition of N-CQDs. Galvanostatic intermittent titration technique is also used to examine Na+ diffusion coefficients.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Environmental pollution and energy crisis are critical issues that require urgent actions, innovative solutions, and global cooperation for a sustainable planet [1–4]. Renewable energy sources offer clean substitutes to fossil fuels, harnessing natural energy sources including wind, water, sun, and heat [5–8]. Energy storage technologies like batteries, supercapacitors, and water-splitting systems are essential for incorporating renewable energy sources into the grid and safeguarding a constant electricity supply [9]. Nevertheless, the existing renewable energy technologies are not sufficient to meet the energy requirement due to the need for high energy and power density storage technologies for electric vehicles [10–12]. Therefore, the development of energy storage and conversion devices with high efficiencies is considered the current research hotspot [13]. Rechargeable lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have recently dominated power source markets due to their high power/energy densities and long-term lifespan [14]. However, the use of LIBs has been limited due to scarce resources and uneven distribution of lithium [15]. Sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) have begun as potential alternatives to LIBs because SIBs are made from more abundant and less toxic materials with similar storage capabilities [16]. In addition, SIBs have long cycle lives, low costs, and high safety performance, making them important for energy storage technologies [17,18]. However, SIBs still face challenges such as poor performance at low temperatures and low energy density/power densities [19]. Recent developments in cathode materials have shown improved storage performance in SIBs [20]. However, the inferior rate capability and stability of anodes in SIBs limits insertion and diffusion of sodium ions due to the large ionic radius [21]. Furthermore, electrode material polarization at higher current densities also reduces the performance of SIBs [22]. Therefore, developing anodes with high capacity, excellent rate performance, and good cyclability is the current focus of research. It is well known that there are three types of anode materials, including conversion, alloy, and intercalation [23]. Among these, conversion-type anodes have shown excellent overall performance with larger theoretical capacities and moderate volume changes [24]. Major materials used for conversion-type anodes of SIBs include metal phosphides, oxides, sulfides, and selenides [25–28]. Among them, metal selenides have demonstrated higher electrical conductivity compared to metal sulfides and metal oxides. Additionally, the sodiation products of NaxSe exhibit superior ionic conductivity compared to NaxS and NaxO [29,30]. Among metal selenides, antimony selenide (Sb2Se3) having a theoretical capacity of 670 mAhg−1 falls under the active metal-active selenide category and has been utilized as an anode material of SIBs [31]. During the sodiation/desodiation process, the conversion and alloy reactions occur in the Sb2Se3-based anodes [32,33]. After the conversion reaction, the products can act as a buffer material in the alloy reaction, which can diminish the stress during particle pulverizations [34]. Despite their good performances, the large volume change during the sodiation/desodiation process leads to serious destructions of the electrode, resulting in a rapid capacity deterioration [35]. Therefore, addressing the volume change is compulsory to achieve promising Sb2Se3 anode materials.

On the other hand, carbon modification is a promising and widely used method to improve the sodium-storage capabilities of anode materials [36]. Significantly, the carbon coating on metal selenide-based anodes can enhance the electrochemical performance by improving electronic conductivity and reducing the volume changes of the anodes. For instance, Hu et al. synthesized Sb2Se3/rGO nanocomposites for the production of a low-temperature anode material, showing good rate capability and stability [34]. Wang et al. demonstrated a sandwich-like SnSe2/reduced graphene oxide (rGO) as an anode material of SIBs, which exhibited an initial Coulombic efficiency (ICE) of 73.7% and a capacity retention of 86.2% at 0.1 Ag−1 after 150 cycles [37]. Previous studies have shown that the addition of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can improve the electrochemical performance of SIBs [38,39]. This sort of nanomaterials can offer several advantages, such as abundant electrochemically active sites, large contact areas with electrolytes, high capacities, superior structural stability, and surface strain resistance supporting shortened Na+ transition distance [40]. To the best of our knowledge, the Sb2Se3/CQDs composites have not been reported for use in the fabrication of SIB anodes. It is promising to explore the feasibility of applying Sb2Se3/CQDs composites as the anode material of SIBs with high performance.

In this study, one-dimensional (1D) Sb2Se3 and CQDs were combined with carbon nanofibers (CNFs) as the anode material of SIBs for the first time. The 1D Sb2Se3 and CQDs were synthesized using the hydrothermal method. Subsequently, the electrospinning method was employed to integrate Sb2Se3 and CQDs into CNFs. Carbon coating using the electrospinning method offers several advantages. These include the capability to achieve uniform and continuous coatings, high surface area, versatility in material integration, cost-effectiveness, and enhanced performance in various applications such as batteries and supercapacitors [41]. Before the electrospinning process, the hydrothermal reaction time was optimized to produce 1D Sb2Se3 with the appropriate size and structure. Generally, 1D nanostructures can provide good thermal stability, electrical conductivity, and mechanical stability due to their unique morphology and geometric characteristics [42]. Significantly, 1D nanofibers feature short diffusion distances, uninterrupted ion, and electron pathways, and improved contact with current collectors, thereby enhancing the rate performance in battery applications [43]. For instance, L. Wu et al. reported the use of Sb–C nanofibers to create an anode for sodium-ion batteries (SIB) and demonstrated their long cycle life and high capacity [44]. Y. Dong et al. developed Sb2Se3@C nanofibers for high-performance LIBs, which exhibited impressive cycling stability and maintained strong performance after 100 charge-discharge cycles [45]. On the other hand, doping CQDs) with heteroatoms such as nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), and phosphorus (P) has attracted significant interest due to the notable improvements in their catalytic, electronic, and optical properties [46]. L. Ruiyi et al. developed an LTO/N,S-GQDs-based anode for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), where the introduction of N,S-GQDs significantly enhanced electron transfer and increased lithium storage capacity [47]. Therefore, the impact of heteroatom doping was also investigated by using nitrogen and sulfur. The N-CQDs and N, S-CQDs were also synthesized and used in the electrospinning process to combine with Sb2Se3. As a result, the Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs anode exhibited a commendable discharge capacity of 647.9 mAhg−1 at 0.05 Ag-1. Additionally, a superior rate capability and stability (72.5% @ 100 cycles) were also achieved for the Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs anode compared to the pristine Sb2Se3 anode (7.1% @ 100 cycles).

Sb2Se3 was synthesized via the hydrothermal process. At first, solution A was prepared by adding 0.3125 g antimony trichloride (SbCl3, Acros) to 37.5 mL of ethylene glycol (C2H4(OH)2, 99.0%, Echo Chemical) and stirred for 1 h. Meanwhile, solution B was prepared by adding 0.25 g of selenium powder (Se, 99.5%, Alfa Aesar) and 0.375 g of sodium borohydride (NaBH4, 98.0%, Showa) to 37.5 mL of deionized water (DIW) and stirred for 30 min. Afterward, solution B was slowly added dropwise into solution A to form an orange-red colored solution, which was continuously stirred. The solution was then transferred into an autoclave and placed in an oven set to 180 °C for carrying out the hydrothermal process with different reaction times. After natural cooling, the products were washed several times with DIW and ethanol using a vacuum filtration method, and then dried in an oven at 60 °C to get the products. The 1D Sb2Se3 synthesized using 8, 24, 30, and 36 h were nominated as Sb2Se3-18 h, Sb2Se3-24 h, Sb2Se3-30 h, and Sb2Se3-36 h, respectively. The schematic illustration of the synthetic process for Sb2Se3 was shown in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of heteroatom-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: A hydrothermal process was used to prepare the CQDs. In the typical process, 4.5 g of citric acid (C6H8O7, 99.0%, Echo Chemical) was added into 50 mL of DIW. Subsequently, 1 g of urea (CH4N2O, 99.0%, Echoe Chemical) or 2 g of L-cysteine (C3H7NO2S, 98.0%, Acros) were added into the above solution. The solution was then transferred into an autoclave and placed in an oven set to 180 °C for 8 h to carry out the hydrothermal process. After natural cooling, the product was filtered using a syringe filter with a pore size of 0.22 μm. A dialyzing membrane with a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 3500 Da was used with changing water for several times to obtain the heteroatom-doped CQDs. The N-CQDs or N, S-CQDs were respectively synthesized by adding urea or L-cysteine in the precursor solution. The schematic illustration of the synthetic process for N-CQDs and N, S-CQDs was shown in Fig. 1.

The electrospinning method was used for the synthesis of Sb2Se3 and Sb2Se3/CQDs incorporated carbon nanofibers (CNFs). Initially, the polymer solution was prepared by dissolving 1.0 g of Sb2Se3 and 0.5 g of polyacrylonitrile (PAN, 100%, Acros) in N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.5%, Echo Chemical). The 10 mg of CQDs (N-CQDs or N, S-CQDs) was added and the stirring process continued for 24 h. The polymer solution was transferred into a 5 mL syringe with a 20-gauge needle, a flow rate of 1 mL/h, and a high applied voltage of 15 kV. The distance between the copper collector and the needle was fixed at 15 cm to collect the nanofibers (Sb2Se3@CQDs/PAN). The collected fibers were then dried in an oven at 60 °C for 6 h. The Sb2Se3@CQDs/PAN nanofibers were pre-oxidized at a heating rate of 1 °C/min to 260 °C and hold for 1 h. The pre-oxidized fibers were then placed in a tubular furnace and carbonized at a heating rate of 2 °C/min to 500 °C under a nitrogen atmosphere and hold for 3 h [48], resulting in the production of Sb2Se3@CQDs/CNFs. The schematic illustration of the synthetic process for Sb2Se3@CQDs/CNFs was shown in Fig. 1. The details about the material and electrochemical measurements were provided in the electronic supplementary information (ESI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Material characterizations of Sb2Se3, CQDs, Sb2Se3/CNFs, Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs and Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs
ce:section.ce:para: To influence of the hydrothermal reaction time on the growth of Sb2Se3 nanorods was firstly investigated. The FESEM was used to examine the structural morphology of the hydrothermally synthesized Sb2Se3. Fig. 2 (a)–(d) shows the SEM images of Sb2Se3 synthesized using the hydrothermal durations of 18, 24, 30 and 36 h, respectively. With the reaction time of 18 h, the nanorod structure was produced and many uncrystallized Sb2Se3 seeds were formed on the surface (Fig. 2(a)). The nanorods become thicker and there are still uncrystallized Sb2Se3 seeds on the surface when the reaction time reaches 24 h (Fig. 2(b)). The seeds on the surface decreased and the thickness of the nanorods increased further when the reaction time reached 30 h (Fig. 2(c)). When the reaction time increased to 36 h, the bigger nanorods were split into smaller nanorods with smooth surfaces (Fig. 2(d)). The growth process of 1D Sb2Se3 nanorods was presented in Fig. 2(e) to clearly illustrate the mechanism. During the hydrothermal process, the Sb-Se complex was first produced and then aggregated to form the nucleus. Subsequently, the nuclei were grown into the Sb2Se3 seeds. As time increases, the Sb2Se3 seeds gradually become nanorod-like structures [49]. Further increasing the hydrothermal duration, the continuously growth rods were split into smaller nanorods in the final stage.

The morphology of Sb2Se3/CNFs, Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs and Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs was also observed by using the SEM images. Fig. 3 (a–d) shows the SEM images of Sb2Se3/CNFs. The Sb2Se3 nanorods prepared using 36 h for the hydrothermal reaction were used for fabricating Sb2Se3/CNFs in the electrospinning process. During the electrospinning process, the nanorod-structured Sb2Se3 was converted into nanofibers with relatively smooth and flat surface. The Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs (Fig. 3(b–e)) and Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs (Fig. 3(c–f)) show rougher surfaces, inferring the attachments of CQDs on the surface of the carbon nanotubes. Furthermore, the morphology of the synthesized Sb2Se3, N-CQDs and N, S-CQDs were examined by the TEM images. The nanorod structure of Sb2Se3 was clearly observed (Fig. 3(g)). Small dot-like structures were observed for the synthesized N-CQDs (Fig. 3(h)), whereas the increased size of the CQDs was obtained for the synthesized N, S-CQDs (Fig. 3(i)). The TEM images were also used to observe the changes before and after the electrospinning process. The TEM image of the Sb2Se3/CNFs was shown in Fig. 3(j). The carbon layers on the Sb2Se3 nanorods indicate the formation of Sb2Se3/CNFs. After the electrospinning process, the Sb2Se3 nanorods were covered by CNFs. Fig. 3(k) and (l) shows the TEM images of Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs and Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs, respectively. The Sb2Se3/CNFs surface becomes rougher after the addition of N-CQDs and N, S-CQDs. The small-sized N-CQDs were not clearly found on the surface of the Sb2Se3/CNFs, whereas the large-sized N, S-CQDs were observed on the Sb2Se3/CNFs surface. These images imply the successful synthesis of Sb2Se3, N-CQDs, N, S-CQDs, Sb2Se3/CNFs, Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs, and Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs.

The composition of Sb2Se3, CQDs, Sb2Se3/CNFs, Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs and Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs was then examined. Fig. 4 (a) shows the XRD patterns of Sb2Se3 synthesized at different hydrothermal durations. The main peaks are located at 17.0°, 24.4°, 27.7°, 34.3° and 41.9°, respectively representing (120), (101), (230), (240), and (250) planes of orthorhombic Sb2Se3 (JCPDS #75–1462) [50]. Although the standard pattern and the synthesized Sb2Se3 respectively show the highest intensity at different planes of (221) and (230), the XRD pattern of the synthesized Sb2Se3 exactly matches the result in the previous report [51]. Also, the abnormally improved intensities of all (hk0) peaks indicate that the rods hold a favored orientation along the direction of (001). Notably, the XRD patterns of Sb2Se3 synthesized using different hydrothermal durations are highly similar, implying the limited influences of hydrothermal durations on the crystallinity and facets of Sb2Se3. In addition, Fig. 4(b) displays the XRD patterns of Sb2Se3, Sb2Se3/CNFs, Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs and Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs. All the peaks are corresponding to Sb2Se3 in all the samples. The additional peaks (star marked) were observed in the XRD patterns of Sb2Se3/CNFs, Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs and Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs, which were fabricated with the electrospinning process involving the pre-oxidation step. These peaks are exactly matched with the standard pattern of Sb2O4 (JCPDS #80–0231) [51]. The Sb2O4 was generated during the pre-oxidation step due to the partial oxidation of Sb2Se3. Also, the peak shift to the lower degrees was observed, due to the increased inter-lattice distances produced by oxides. Notably, the narrow and sharp peaks reveal the high crystallinity of the samples. However, XRD patterns show no obvious peaks corresponding to CQDs. Therefore, Raman spectroscopy was used to examine the existence of N-CQDs and N, S-CQDs in the synthesized composite materials. Meanwhile, the formation of CNFs in the electrospinning process was also examined by Raman spectroscopy. Fig. 4(c) depicts the Raman spectra of Sb2Se3, Sb2Se3/CNFs, Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs and Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs. Generally, the Raman spectrum of carbon nanomaterials exhibits D and G bands representing the defects in the carbon lattice and in-plane vibration of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms, respectively [52]. The ID/IG ratio provides information about the nature of disorder and the relative amounts of sp2 and sp³ hybridized carbon atoms. It is important to note that the ID/IG and sp2/sp³ ratios are not directly calculated from each other. The higher ID/IG ratio indicates an increase in disorder within the carbon material and a higher proportion of sp³ hybridized carbon relative to sp2, that is, a higher sp2/sp³ ratio. No D and G bands were observed in the spectrum of Sb2Se3, due to the absence of the carbon nanomaterial. However, obvious D and G bands respectively raised at 1370 and 1594 cm−1 in the spectra of Sb2Se3/CNFs, Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs, and Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs, implying the formation of CNFs and the existences of CQDs in the composite material. Furthermore, the ID/IG ratios of 2.70, 2.88, and 2.88 were respectively calculated for the Sb2Se3/CNFs, Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs and Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs. A higher ID/IG ratio of Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs and Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs indicates that the material has more defects and can possibly provide more active sites which benefits electrochemical performance. Moreover, the XPS technique was used to analyze the elemental composition of materials and their valence states [53]. The XPS survey spectra of Sb2Se3, Sb2Se3/CNFs, Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs, and Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs were displayed in Fig. 4(d). The signals of C, N, Sb and Se elements appeared in the spectra of all samples. The signal of the S element appears only in the XPS spectrum of Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs, indicating the existence of sulfur-doped CQDs. Notably, the intensities of the C 1s peak in the spectra of Sb2Se3/CNFs, Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs, and Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs are much stronger than Sb2Se3, suggesting the successful formation of the composite containing CNFs. In addition, Figs. S1(a)–(b) respectively displays the core spectra of Sb 3d + O 1s and Se 3d for Sb2Se3. The Sb 3d + O 1s spectrum shows two significant peaks at 528.46 and 537.30 eV respectively attributed to the existence of Sb III and V [54]. The peak at 529.94 eV corresponding to the O 1s spectrum indicates the Sb-O bond formed by atmospheric oxygen on the surface of Sb2Se3. Also, the core spectrum of Se 3d exhibiting two peaks at 54.64 and 55.62 eV were assigned to Se2− in Sb2Se3 [55]. Similarly, the core level Sb 3d + O 1s spectrum of Sb2Se3/CNF depicts the peaks at 528.18 and 537.59 eV respectively for Sb III and V (Fig. S2(a)). After the electrospinning process, the peaks at 529.9 and 530.96 eV respectively ascribing to the Sb-O-C and C-O-C bonds were observed in the spectrum of Sb2Se3/CNF, due to the coating of CNFs on the Sb2Se3 in the pre-oxidation process. The C 1s spectrum of Sb2Se3/CNFs shows the peaks at 283.94, 285.07, and 287.46 eV respectively representing the sp2-carbon, sp3-carbon, and C=O bonds of CNFs (Fig. S2(b)). Fig. 4(a)–(d) shows the core level Sb 3d + O 1s, Se 3d, C 1s and N 1s spectra of Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs. The core level spectrum of Sb 3d + O 1s showing the peaks at 528.19 and 537.56 eV respectively confirm the existence of Sb III and V. The Sb-O-C peak at 530 eV confirms the carbon coating, whereas the C-O-C peak at 530.98 eV indicates the addition of N-CQDs. A similar XPS pattern was observed for Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs, as shown in Fig. S3. Additionally, the peak at 167 eV assigning to the C-S-H bond in the S 2p spectrum confirms the S doping in the CQDs.

The electrochemical rate performance of Sb2Se3 synthesized at different hydrothermal durations was first examined at the current densities of 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.8, and 1.6 Ag-1. The specific capacity as a function of cycle number is shown in Fig. S4(a). At a low current density of 0.05 A/g, the specific capacities of all anodes are close to the theoretical value (∼670 mAhg−1). However, at higher current densities, the Sb2Se3-18 h, Sb2Se3-24 h, and Sb2Se3-30 h anodes show higher capacities than that of the Sb2Se3-36 h anode, and the highest specific capacities were obtained by the Sb2Se3-30 h anode. As observed from the SEM images, the Sb2Se3 nanorods synthesized at 18, 24, and 30 h possess a larger cross-sectional area, which may lead to smaller resistances and higher conductivities. The rod-like structure gradually split into small nanorods when the reaction time increases to 36 h, resulting in a smaller cross-sectional area and hence larger resistances. Also, the Sb2Se3-36 h may possess higher crystallinity due to the complete reaction. Generally, the conductivity decreases as crystallinity increases because an ordered crystalline structure hinders the ion transfer process. As a result, the specific capacity at higher current densities is inferior for the Sb2Se3-36 h anode. Finally, when the current density returns to 0.05 Ag-1, the Sb2Se3-36 h anode shows the best specific capacity. This unexpected result may be due to the highest crystallinity of Sb2Se3-36 h to display the best stability. Therefore, regarding to the excellent stability, Sb2Se3-36 h was used in the electrospinning process to prepare the composite materials. On the other hand, Fig. S4(b) shows the rate performance of the Sb2Se3, Sb2Se3/CNFs, Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs, and Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs anodes measured at the current densities of 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.8 and 1.6 Ag-1. The Sb2Se3 anode performs better specific capacities at low current densities, but its specific capacities decreased seriously when the current density increased to 1.6 Ag-1. Also, in the last five cycles with the current density of 0.05 Ag-1, the largely reduced specific capacity of the Sb2Se3 anode indicates its poor stability. On the other hand, the Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs anode delivered the best rate performance with the discharge capacities of 647.9, 571.4, 507.2, 424.5, 316.5, and 179.5 mAhg−1 respectively at the current densities of 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.8, and 1.6 Ag-1. With the current density returning to 0.05 Ag-1, the specific capacity of the Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs anode still maintained 589.95 mAhg−1, indicating its better Na+ storage capacity than all other electrodes. In addition, the electrochemical performance of the Sb2Se3, Sb2Se3/CNFs, Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs, and Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs anodes was examined by the CV curves at the scan rate of 0.1 mV/s in the potential window of 0–2.5 V vs. Na+/Na. Fig. 5 (a) shows the CV curves of Sb2Se3 measured for 5 cycles. The reduction peak at 1.10 V vs. Na+/Na in the first cycle was generated due to the SEI film formation. The peaks at 0.80 and 0.25 V vs. Na+/Na are respectively for the transformation and alloying reactions. During the reverse scan, the oxidation peaks at 0.91 and 1.48 V are due to the de-alloying and reverse transformation reactions. A shift in the peak potential was observed in the 2nd cycle, and the peak potential remained stable during the 2nd to 5th cycles. Notably, the gradually shrunk CV curve indicates the decreased stability of Sb2Se3 in continuous cycles. The conversion (Eqs. (1) and (2)) and alloying reaction (Eq. (3)) mechanisms were provided in the following equations [56].
(1) Sb2O4 + 8Na+ + 8e− → 2Sb + 4Na2O (1.5 V vs. Na/Na+)
(2) Sb2Se3 + 6Na+ + 6e− → 2Sb + 3Na2Se (1.0 V vs. Na/Na+)
(3) 2Sb + 6Na+ + 6e− → 2Na3Sb (0.3 V vs. Na/Na+)

On the other hand, similar CV curves with slight peak shifts were observed for the Sb2Se3/CNFs, Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs and Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs anodes. In comparison with Sb2Se3, there is an extra reduction peak raised at 1.45 V for Sb2Se3/CNFs in the 2nd to 5th cycles, which is attributed to the formation of Sb2O4 due to the pre-oxidation process (Fig. 5(b)). In addition, the same peak area was maintained from 1st to 5th cycles, indicating the stability improvement due to the coating of CNFs. As it is widely known, the carbon layer plays a significant role in increasing electronic conductivity, which is favorable for improving rate performances [57]. Moreover, in the cases of Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs (Fig. 5(c)) and Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs (Fig. 5(d)), there are no significant changes in the peak area even after the addition of CQDs. Owing to the formation of defects, the addition of CQDs may cause a slight decrease in stability. Nevertheless, the existence of active sites can help sodium ions to diffuse into the nanofibers and react with active materials. As a result, the higher capacity can be obtained without sacrificing too much stability.

The CV curves were then measured by using different scan rates to examine the pattern of electrochemical charge carrier transfer with the equation of i = aνb, where ν is the scan rate, i is the peak current, and a and b are fitting parameters. The electrochemical behavior of electrodes could be either battery behavior (diffusion controlled; b = 0.5) or pseudo-capacitive behavior (surface controlled; b = 1) [58]. Fig. 5(e)–(h) shows the CV curves of the Sb2Se3, Sb2Se3/CNFs, Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs and Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs anodes at the scan rates of 0.5, 0.7, 0.9 and 1.5 mV/s. The peak current responses and area of the CV curve increase with increasing scan rates. To calculate the b values, the log of peak 1 and peak 2 current values were plotted versus log of the scan rates. The corresponding plots of the Sb2Se3, Sb2Se3/CNFs, Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs, and Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs anodes were presented in Figs. S5(a)–(d), respectively. The b values of 0.89 (peak 1) and 0.64 (peak 2) were calculated for Sb2Se3, revealing that the charge carrier transport belongs to the capacitive behavior. Moreover, the b values are slightly decreased for Sb2Se3/CNFs (0.72, 0.59), Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs (0.67, 0.52), and Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs (0.66, 0.57) compared with those of Sb2Se3. In the anodes with CNFs, Na+ ions need to diffuse into the internal active material through the CNFs outer layer during the charge and discharge process. Similarly, the addition of CQDs may hinder the diffusion of Na+ ions from CNFs to the internal active material. As a result, the b values decreased and the behavior is biased towards the battery behavior for Sb2Se3/CNFs, Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs and Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs. Furthermore, the Galvanostatic charge/discharge (GC/D) test was performed to evaluate the electrochemical performance. Fig. 5(i)–(l) shows the GC/D curves of Sb2Se3, Sb2Se3/CNFs, Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs and Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs anodes recorded at 0.2 A/g, respectively. The GC/D curves are matched with their corresponding CV curves. As shown in Fig. 5(i), the 1st discharge curve of Sb2Se3 featured three plateaus located at 1.16, 0.96 and 0.46 V vs. Na+/Na. As for the charging process, the plateaus were located at 0.72 and 2.18 V. Similarly, the Sb2Se3/CNFs, Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs and Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs anodes show three discharge plateaus at similar potentials and one charge plateau at 0.72 V. Furthermore, the specific capacity of 783 mAhg−1 was achieved at the first discharge cycle and drastically reduced to 45.97 mAhg−1 after the 100th cycle for the Sb2Se3 anode. However, the discharge capacity retentions are higher for the Sb2Se3/CNFs, Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs and Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs anodes in comparison with the pristine Sb2Se3 after the 100th cycle. Among them, the Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs anode shows the highest discharge capacity of 743.49 mAhg−1 at the 1st cycle and maintained 413.6 mAhg−1 after the 100th cycle.

The capacitance retention of the anodes was examined for 100 continued charge-discharge cycles at the current density of 0.2 Ag-1. The corresponding result was presented in Fig. 6 (a). After 100 cycles, the capacitance of Sb2Se3 is only 7.1% of the original value, and the Coulombic efficiency is unstable. It is speculated that sodium ions emigrating through Sb2Se3 causes the material cracking, which can generate higher contact area to induce the rapid capacitance changes. On the other hand, Sb2Se3/CNFs and Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs anodes maintained the best stability after 100 cycles with the highest capacity retention rate reaching 78.8% and 72.5%, respectively. Although the capacitance retention of the Sb2Se3/CNFs anode is higher, it can be found that the capacity of the Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs anode after 100 cycles is higher than that of the Sb2Se3/CNFs anode due to the uniform dispersion of N-CQDs. Meanwhile, the capacity retention of the Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs anode is only 66.0% due to the uneven particle sizes and severe aggregations of N, S-CQDs. In addition, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was used to explore the electrode reaction kinetics and the reaction at the electrode/electrolyte interface. Fig. 6(b) and (c) respectively shows the Nyquist plots of the Sb2Se3, Sb2Se3/CNFs, Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs, and Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs anodes after the 1st and the 100th cycles. In addition, the insert figure is the equivalent circuit used to fit the Nyquist plots, where the Rs, Rct, Zw, and CPE are referred to the solution resistance, charge-transfer resistance, Warburg diffusion process, and constant phase element, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6(b), all the Nyquist plots displayed two semicircles, which in the high-frequency region is attributed to the SEI formation and that in the mid-frequency region is related to the charge-transfer impedance. The charge-transfer impedance is considered as an opposite index of the electrode conductivity. The Rct values measured after the 1st cycle are 685.3, 630.3, 310.0, and 181.0 Ω respectively for the Sb2Se3, Sb2Se3/CNFs, Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs and Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs anodes. These results reveal that adding carbon nanomaterials can enhance the conductivity of the electrodes. After the 100th cycle, the charge-transfer resistance was examined again (Fig. 6(c)). The Rct values of 352.3, 124.0, 70.1, and 54.0 Ω were respectively obtained for the Sb2Se3, Sb2Se3/CNFs, Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs, and Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs anodes. The charge-transfer resistance was reduced after 100 cycles, due to the activation of anode materials. Also, the Nyquist plot of the Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs anode shows two semicircles in the first cycle, and there are still two semicircles remained in the 100th cycle. This phenomenon is possibly caused by the growth of the SEI layer which can effectively improves the electron transfer. Further analysis was conducted through Galvanostatic Intermittent Titration (GITT) measurements to explore the Na+ diffusion coefficient (DNa +). The DNa + value of the electrodes was calculated using Eq. (4) as follows.

where τ represents the time for applying a constant current, MB is the molar mass of the active material mB is the mass of the active material, S is the electrode-electrolyte interface area, VM is the molar volume of the active material, ΔEτ is the change in overall voltage under a constant current, and ΔEs is the change in the potential chansge at each step [59]. Fig. 6(d) shows the GITT curves of Sb2Se3, Sb2Se3/CNFs, Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs and Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs anodes from 0 to 2.5 V vs. Na/Na+. The calculated log(D) of Sb2Se3, Sb2Se3/CNFs, Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs and Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs anodes for the sodiation and desodiation process was presented in Fig. S6. The GITT study reveals that the Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs anode has the highest diffusion coefficient during sodium ion intercalation and migration, due to the high conductivity of N-CQDs and CNFs.

To fairly evaluate the electrochemical performance of Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs proposed in this work, the rate capability and stability of the Sb and Sb2Se3-based anode materials reported in previous literatures and this work were listed in Table 1 . Typically, the cycling stability was tested for 100 cycles. Various carbon nanomaterials, such as reduced graphene oxide (rGO), carbon nanotube (CNT), nitrogen-doped graphene (N-graphene), porous carbon and carbon were used to combine with Sb2Se3 for the fabrication of anodes. It was noticed that the effect of CQDs addition with Sb2Se3@CNFs was systematically studied for the first time in this work. In the present work, the Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs anode even exhibits a better rate capability than the Sb2Se3@CNFs anode. Generally, the specific capacity and rate capability of the Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs are better than those reported in previous literatures, but the stability performance is slightly poorer than those reported in the related systems. More efficient carbon materials are expected to integrate in this anode for further improving the long-term stability.

In summary, the Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs were obtained using the electrospinning method. By optimizing the hydrothermal reaction time, smaller-sized Sb2Se3 nanorods with smooth surfaces were achieved. The Sb2Se3 nanorods were observed to be growing toward the [001] direction. The Sb2Se3-36 h exhibited the highest specific capacity due to its highest crystallinity. However, it showed poor stability and retained only 7.1% of the initial capacity after 100 cycles because of the large volume expansion. To address the volume expansion issue and improve stability, Sb2Se3/CNFs, Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs, and Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs were prepared through the electrospinning process. Notably, Sb2Se3/CNFs demonstrated a high capacity retention of 78.8% after 100 cycles. Despite the improved capacity retention, Sb2Se3/CNFs showed a lower specific capacity than Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs after 100 cycles. The uniformly dispersed N-CQDs effectively enhanced the capacity performance of Sb2Se3/CNFs. However, the Sb2Se3@N, S-CQDs/CNFs exhibited a low capacity retention of 66.0% due to uneven particle sizes and severe aggregations of N, S-CQDs. On the other hand, Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs demonstrated the highest reversible capacity of 647.9 mAhg−1 at 0.05 Ag−1and maintained 444.7 mAhg−1 even at a higher rate of 0.4 Ag-1. The stability study revealed that the Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs anode retained 72.5% after 100 cycles. The addition of carbon coating and N-CQDs successfully reduced the volume expansion and improved the electrode conductivity, resulting in the superior electrochemical performance for Sb2Se3@N-CQDs/CNFs.
